title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a wildlife filmmaker called Bella Thorne talking about the challenges of filming animals in their natural habitat. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a wildlife filmmaker called Bella Thorne talking about the challenges of filming animals in their natural habitat. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Bella Thorne discusses the patience and technology required to capture rare animal behaviors on camera.
extracts.audio_script:
Good morning. I’m Bella. People think my job is all about traveling to exotic locations, but mostly it’s about sitting very still in a small wooden box for fourteen hours a day. I’m a wildlife filmmaker. My interest in nature didn't start with a trip to Africa; it actually began when I spent hours watching a [7]spider’s web[/7] in my garden during a summer holiday as a child. I was fascinated by the precision of the hunt. I realized that if you wait long enough, the natural world reveals incredible stories.

The biggest challenge in our job is remaining invisible. We use 'blinds' or 'hides' to stay out of sight. I’ve found that [8]scent masking[/8] is the most important part of our setup. If a leopard smells a human, it won't behave naturally, or worse, it might come looking for you. We have to be incredibly careful about the soaps and detergents we use before we go into the field. You have to blend into the environment not just visually, but also olfactorily.

Technology has changed how we film at night. We used to have to use bright spotlights, which obviously disturbed the animals. Now, we use thermal imaging cameras. I noticed that the [9]heat signature[/9] of a predator against the cold ground allows us to film behavior that has never been seen before. We recently captured a pride of lions hunting in total darkness, and the level of coordination they showed was breathtaking. It’s like having a superpower.

Then there’s the 'macro' world—the tiny insects and plants. I’ve found that [10]vibration sensors[/10] are essential when filming in the rainforest. They alert us to the movement of tiny creatures long before we can see them with our eyes. It allows us to get the camera in position without startling the animal. It’s a slow, methodical process that requires an incredible amount of patience.

One of the most difficult things to film is animal communication. We use highly sensitive microphones. I was surprised to find that [11]low-frequency rumbles[/11] from elephants can travel for miles through the ground. By recording these sounds, we can understand the social structure of the herd in a way that visual filming alone can't provide. It’s about capturing the full sensory experience of the wild.

Safety is a constant concern. I’ve found that the [12]behavioral cues[/12] of the animals are the best indicator of when a situation is becoming dangerous. You have to learn to read the subtle flick of a tail or the way an ear moves. If you miss those signs, you are putting your life at risk. You have to respect that you are a guest in their world.

Looking back, the biggest change in the industry is the focus on conservation. We no longer just want to show pretty pictures; we want to highlight the threats these animals face. I believe that [13]emotional storytelling[/13] is more effective than just providing facts. If the audience cares about a specific individual animal, they are much more likely to support efforts to protect its habitat.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]mentorship program[/14] for young filmmakers from the countries where we film. I want to give them the tools to tell their own stories about their local wildlife. They have a perspective that we, as outsiders, can never truly have. Thank you for your time.
